tldr: Evaluates Gemma 3 12B, Gemini 2.5 Flash, and Gemini 3.1 Pro on a custom algebraic-transcendental math benchmark, extracting telemetry to measure accuracy, latency, cost, and novel cognitive metrics like mode collapse and inhibitory control.
summary: This notebook benchmarks three Google LLMs on a deterministic algebraic-transcendental problem set designed to trap models in repetitive hallucinations. It generates 100 synthetic prompts with strict mathematical routing, runs them via the kaggle_benchmarks API, and extracts detailed telemetry (latency, tokens, cost, TPS) from execution logs. The author introduces novel cognitive metrics—Implicit Calibration Error (ICE), Domain-Switching Cost (DSC), Compute Yield Ratio (CYR), and a Rambling Index—to quantify executive function flaws like mode collapse and working memory decay. Results show that while deep reasoning models scale compute dynamically to improve accuracy, they remain bottlenecked by token-based floating-point approximation rather than true arithmetic execution.
extensive_summary:
## Dataset Generation & Benchmark Setup
The notebook constructs a 100-sample "Algebraic Gauntlet" dataset using Python's random and math libraries. Each sample defines variables A and B, computes intermediate values X and Y via modulo operations, and routes through three conditional branches based on trigonometric and divisibility checks. Each branch applies complex algebraic-transcendental formulas (involving powers, exponentials, logarithms, trigonometric functions). A cross-domain modifier converts the absolute integer part of the result to binary and octal, counting zeros and summing digits to adjust the final target to 6 decimal places. Prompts enforce strict formatting.

## Evaluation & Telemetry Extraction
Models are evaluated using kaggle_benchmarks with a custom grader that extracts numbers via regex and checks against the target using math.isclose with rel_tol=1e-7. The notebook runs google/gemma-3-12b, google/gemini-2.5-flash, and google/gemini-3.1-pro-preview. Execution logs (*run.json) are parsed to extract model version, raw output, target answer, wall-clock duration, input/output tokens, backend latency, and cost. A relaxed 4-decimal tolerance grader is also applied for comparison.

## Novel Cognitive Metrics
The author calculates four executive function metrics from the telemetry:
- Implicit Calibration Error (ICE): (1 - Accuracy) * Error_Confidence, penalizing models that repeatedly output the same wrong answer (mode collapse).
- Domain-Switching Cost (DSC): Ratio of average output tokens on failed prompts vs. passed prompts, measuring flexibility during abrupt rule changes.
- Compute Yield Ratio (CYR): Accuracy (%) / Average Output Tokens (in thousands), quantifying working memory efficiency.
- Rambling Index: Average extra characters generated beyond the strict output format, measuring inhibitory control.

## Results & Analysis
Gemini 3.1 Pro achieved 53% accuracy with low mode collapse (8%) and high inhibitory control, displaying a heavy-tailed latency distribution indicative of dynamic System-2 test-time compute scaling. Gemma 3 12B and Gemini 2.5 Flash showed rigid, low-variance latency spikes (System-1 behavior) and high mode collapse (68% and 42%, respectively). Relaxed grading improved accuracy, revealing the FPU Ceiling: models understand routing but fail arithmetic due to token approximation compounding errors in transcendental functions. The notebook concludes that true AI progress requires robust executive functions beyond mere compute scaling.
critical_findings: LLMs lack deterministic FPUs and rely on token approximation, causing catastrophic floating-point errors when transcendental functions are chained.
Fast models exhibit rigid Gaussian latency spikes (System-1), while reasoning models show heavy-tailed latency distributions (System-2) indicating dynamic test-time compute scaling.
Mode collapse correlates strongly with low inhibitory control, as models repeatedly output identical hallucinated strings when failing.
notable_techniques: Implicit Calibration Error (ICE) metric to quantify mode collapse and hallucination stubbornness
Dual-tolerance grading (strict 6-decimal vs relaxed 4-decimal) to isolate arithmetic approximation errors
Telemetry extraction from Kaggle benchmark JSON logs for latency, token, cost, and TPS analysis
Heavy-tailed latency distribution plotting to distinguish System-1 heuristics from System-2 test-time compute scaling
Domain-Switching Cost (DSC) and Compute Yield Ratio (CYR) metrics to measure cognitive flexibility and working memory efficiency
